ticket.description: Applications take several minutes to open after the user signs in.

actions.label: Ask whether only this user/device is affected
actions.result: The user says only this workstation is slow. Other Accounting users are working normally.

evidence.title: Scope
evidence.body: Only one workstation appears affected. This points away from a department-wide outage.

actions.label: Open Task Manager and check resource usage
actions.result: Task Manager shows Disk at 100%, CPU at 7%, and Memory at 43%.

evidence.title: Task Manager
evidence.body: Disk is saturated at 100%; CPU and memory are not the bottleneck.

actions.label: Sort processes by disk usage
actions.result: A backup sync client is constantly retrying a failed job and consuming disk I/O.

evidence.title: Root Cause
evidence.body: The backup client retry loop is causing constant disk activity.

actions.label: Pause the backup client and clear the failed job
actions.result: The backup client stops retrying the failed job. Disk activity returns to normal.

evidence.title: Fix Applied
evidence.body: The backup client retry loop was stopped and the failed job was cleared.

actions.label: Verify applications open normally
actions.result: Applications now open normally and disk usage remains low.

evidence.title: Verification
evidence.body: Performance returned to normal after correcting the backup client issue.

actions.label: Document symptoms, root cause, fix, and verification
actions.result: Ticket notes include symptoms, evidence, root cause, action taken, and verification results.

actions.label: Replace the RAM
actions.result: RAM replacement does not improve performance because memory usage was not high.

penalty.reason: Replacing RAM was too invasive and not supported by the evidence.

actions.label: Perform a clean Windows installation
actions.result: This would be highly disruptive and is not justified before identifying the bottleneck.

penalty.reason: A clean install is too invasive before basic troubleshooting and verification.

grading.summary: A strong troubleshooting path scopes the issue, checks resource usage, identifies disk saturation, fixes the responsible process, verifies performance, and documents the ticket.

ticket.description: Browser homepage changes by itself, search results redirect, and pop-ups appear after login.

actions.label: Ask what changed before the issue started
actions.result: The user installed a free coupon browser extension yesterday.

evidence.title: User Interview
evidence.body: Symptoms started after installing a suspicious browser extension.

actions.label: Quarantine the system from the network
actions.result: The laptop is isolated from the network to reduce potential spread and unwanted traffic.

actions.label: Disable System Restore before remediation
actions.result: System Restore is disabled so malware cannot be restored from old restore points.

actions.label: Remove suspicious extension and run updated anti-malware scan
actions.result: The suspicious extension is removed and the scan removes unwanted software.

evidence.title: Anti-malware Results
evidence.body: Potentially unwanted browser software was removed. Browser settings were reset.

actions.label: Schedule scans and confirm security updates
actions.result: Future scans are scheduled and signatures are updated.

actions.label: Re-enable System Restore and create a clean restore point
actions.result: System Restore is re-enabled and a clean restore point is created.

actions.label: Educate the user about browser extensions and downloads
actions.result: The user is advised to avoid untrusted extensions and bundled installers.

actions.label: Document symptoms, containment, remediation, and education
actions.result: Ticket includes symptoms, actions taken, scan results, and user education.

actions.label: Immediately wipe and reinstall Windows
actions.result: This may work eventually but is too invasive before following the malware removal process.

penalty.reason: A clean install should not be the first response to browser hijacker symptoms.

grading.summary: The malware-removal path should identify symptoms, quarantine, disable restore, remediate, schedule scans/updates, re-enable restore, educate the user, and document the work.

ticket.description: The user forgot their password and cannot sign in.

actions.label: Confirm whether this is a domain account
actions.result: The device is joined to the CORP domain and the user signs in with a domain account.

evidence.title: Account Type
evidence.body: The user account is managed centrally in Active Directory or the approved identity tool.

actions.label: Verify the user's identity according to policy
actions.result: The user identity is verified through the approved help desk process.

actions.label: Reset the domain password in the approved identity tool
actions.result: The domain password is reset.

actions.label: Require password change at next sign-in
actions.result: The user must set a new private password at next sign-in.

actions.label: Verify the user can sign in and change the password
actions.result: The user signs in successfully and creates a new password.

actions.label: Document identity verification and password reset completion
actions.result: Ticket is documented without recording the user's password.

actions.label: Ask the user for their old password
actions.result: Users should never be asked to disclose passwords.

penalty.reason: Asking for a user's password violates security best practices.

grading.summary: A secure password reset requires identifying the account type, verifying identity, resetting through the approved identity system, requiring change at sign-in, verifying access, and documenting without storing passwords.

ticket.description: The user sent several labels to the network printer, but nothing prints.

actions.label: Ask if other users can print to the same printer
actions.result: Other users can print. The issue appears isolated to this workstation.

evidence.title: Scope
evidence.body: Only one workstation is affected; the printer itself is likely available.

actions.label: Verify the printer is powered on and online
actions.result: The printer is online and has paper and toner.

evidence.title: Printer Status
evidence.body: The network printer is online and ready.

actions.label: Check the local print queue
actions.result: Several label jobs are stuck in the queue with an error status.

evidence.title: Print Queue
evidence.body: Jobs are stuck locally, pointing to a print queue or spooler issue.

actions.label: Restart the Print Spooler service and clear stuck jobs
actions.result: The stuck jobs clear and the Print Spooler restarts successfully.

actions.label: Print a test page or test label
actions.result: The test label prints successfully.

evidence.title: Verification
evidence.body: A test label printed successfully after restarting the spooler.

actions.label: Document queue issue, spooler restart, and successful test print
actions.result: The ticket includes the symptom, root cause, fix, and verification.

actions.label: Replace the printer immediately
actions.result: Replacing the printer is unnecessary because other users can print and the printer is online.

penalty.reason: Replacing hardware before checking scope and queue state is too invasive.

actions.label: Reinstall Windows on the workstation
actions.result: This is disruptive and not justified for a stuck print queue.

penalty.reason: A clean install is not appropriate for a routine printer queue issue.

grading.summary: A good printer path scopes the issue, checks printer status, checks the queue, restarts the spooler when appropriate, verifies printing, and documents the work.

ticket.description: One secure website displays 'Your connection is not private.' Other websites load normally.

actions.label: Ask whether other websites work
actions.result: Other websites load normally. The issue appears related to HTTPS validation for the secure site.

evidence.title: Scope
evidence.body: General connectivity works. The symptom points toward certificate validation rather than total network failure.

actions.label: Check the system date, time, and time zone
actions.result: The laptop date is set two years in the past.

evidence.title: System Clock
evidence.body: The incorrect date can make valid certificates appear expired or not yet valid.

actions.label: Correct the date, time, and time zone
actions.result: The laptop is set to the correct date, time, and time zone.

actions.label: Reopen the secure site and verify the certificate warning is gone
actions.result: The secure site opens normally without certificate warnings.

evidence.title: Verification
evidence.body: Correcting the system clock resolved the certificate warning.

actions.label: Document incorrect system clock and verification
actions.result: Ticket notes explain the incorrect date/time and successful retest.

actions.label: Disable browser certificate warnings
actions.result: This hides a security warning instead of fixing the root cause.

penalty.reason: Disabling certificate warnings is unsafe and not a valid fix.

actions.label: Run ipconfig /flushdns and close the ticket
actions.result: DNS cache cleanup does not resolve a certificate validity warning caused by time drift.

penalty.reason: DNS cache issues usually cause name resolution problems, not certificate date validation errors.

grading.summary: Certificate warnings often require checking the system clock before making invasive browser or network changes.

ticket.description: After a firmware update, the laptop boots to a BitLocker recovery key screen.

actions.label: Verify the user's identity according to policy
actions.result: The user's identity is verified through the approved process.

actions.label: Verify the device asset tag and ownership
actions.result: The asset tag matches the user's assigned laptop in inventory.

evidence.title: Asset Verification
evidence.body: Device ownership was confirmed before releasing recovery material.

actions.label: Retrieve the BitLocker recovery key from the approved management system
actions.result: The recovery key is retrieved from the approved system and provided according to policy.

actions.label: Verify the laptop boots and BitLocker protection resumes
actions.result: The laptop boots successfully and BitLocker protection is active.

evidence.title: Verification
evidence.body: System booted successfully after recovery key entry and protection remains enabled.

actions.label: Document firmware update as likely trigger and record verification
actions.result: Ticket documents the trigger, key retrieval process, and successful boot verification.

actions.label: Explain not to store recovery keys insecurely
actions.result: The user is reminded not to write recovery keys in unsecured notes or messages.

actions.label: Disable BitLocker permanently to prevent future prompts
actions.result: This removes full-disk protection from a Finance laptop.

penalty.reason: Disabling BitLocker permanently weakens data protection and does not address policy requirements.

actions.label: Provide the recovery key without verifying identity or asset ownership
actions.result: Recovery keys are sensitive and must not be released without verification.

penalty.reason: Recovery key release requires identity and device verification.

grading.summary: BitLocker recovery must protect sensitive recovery material while restoring access and documenting the trigger and verification.

ticket.description: After a Windows update, the user can sign in but only sees a black screen with a movable mouse pointer.

actions.label: Ask what changed before the issue started
actions.result: The user says the issue started immediately after a Windows update last night.

evidence.title: Recent Change
evidence.body: The symptom began after a Windows update, suggesting a driver or update issue.

actions.label: Boot into Safe Mode or Windows Recovery startup settings
actions.result: The system loads in Safe Mode using minimal drivers.

actions.label: Roll back or uninstall the recent display driver/update
actions.result: The recently updated display driver is rolled back.

evidence.title: Driver Action
evidence.body: A display driver rollback was performed from Safe Mode.

actions.label: Restart normally and verify desktop loads
actions.result: The workstation boots normally and the desktop loads.

actions.label: Pause or block the problematic driver update according to policy
actions.result: The problematic update is paused while the issue is reviewed.

actions.label: Document update, Safe Mode recovery, rollback, and verification
actions.result: Ticket includes recent change, recovery steps, fix, and verification.

actions.label: Replace the graphics card immediately
actions.result: There is not enough evidence of hardware failure. The issue started after an update.

penalty.reason: Replacing hardware before Safe Mode/driver rollback is too invasive.

actions.label: Perform a clean installation of Windows
actions.result: A clean install is not the least-invasive first response when Safe Mode and rollback are available.

penalty.reason: Clean install should be a last resort, not the first recovery step.

grading.summary: A strong recovery path identifies the recent update, uses Safe Mode, rolls back the likely driver/update, verifies normal boot, prevents recurrence, and documents the work.

ticket.description: Corporate email stopped syncing on the phone after the user changed their password.

actions.label: Confirm whether the issue started after a password change
actions.result: The user changed their password this morning and email stopped syncing shortly after.

evidence.title: Recent Change
evidence.body: Email sync failure started after a password change.

actions.label: Verify Wi-Fi or cellular data connectivity
actions.result: The phone has working internet access. Websites load normally.

actions.label: Update the saved email account password on the phone
actions.result: The stored password is updated in the email account settings.

actions.label: Send and receive a test email
actions.result: The phone receives new mail and sends a test message successfully.

evidence.title: Verification
evidence.body: Test send/receive succeeded after updating saved credentials.

actions.label: Explain that mobile apps may need updated credentials after password changes
actions.result: The user understands why mobile mail prompted after the password change.

actions.label: Document password-change cause and successful sync test
actions.result: Ticket notes include the password change, credential update, and verification.

actions.label: Factory reset the phone immediately
actions.result: This is unnecessarily disruptive for a likely saved-credential issue.

penalty.reason: Factory reset is too invasive before checking saved credentials and connectivity.

actions.label: Change the APN settings
actions.result: APN settings are not indicated because internet connectivity works and the issue followed a password change.

penalty.reason: APN is for carrier data access, not a password-related email sync failure.

grading.summary: When email stops syncing after a password change, verify connectivity, update saved credentials, test sync, educate the user, and document the fix.
